title: Preparing a reed pen from bamboo or river reed
subtitle: The oldest writing tool still in active use
excerpt: A reed pen, cut from bamboo cane or dried river reed, produces a wide, slightly rough stroke that suits large-scale calligraphy and bold lettering. The preparation is simpler than cutting a quill and the resulting pen is more durable. Used in the Islamic calligraphic tradition and in Western broad-edge calligraphy practice since antiquity.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), tools and materials section, Johnston discusses reed pens alongside quills and metal nibs, noting their use in Eastern traditions. The preparation method described here follows the classical Arabic calligraphy tradition documented in pre-1928 sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A writing pen made from a section of bamboo cane, river reed (Phragmites), or similar hollow-stemmed plant, cut and split at the tip to form a broad-edge or pointed writing nib.
The Arabic and Turkish term for a reed pen; the traditional instrument of Islamic calligraphy, cut from Arundo donax (giant reed) and shaped by the calligrapher themselves.

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Use sharp blades only; change when they dull. Bamboo splits unpredictably: cut slowly.

content.text: predates the quill by several thousand years. Egyptian scribes used Phragmites reed cut and split across the tip; the Islamic calligraphic tradition refined the technique into the
. The bamboo version is easier to source and cut in a home studio, and produces a broad writing edge suited to large-scale Foundational or italic practice work.

items.name: Dried bamboo cane, 12 to 20 mm diameter, cut into sections 20 to 25 cm long
items.substitutions: Dried giant reed (Arundo donax); dried river cane; bamboo garden stakes trimmed to length

content.text: Choose the end of the section closer to the node (the solid joint in the bamboo); this end will be the writing end.

Make the first cut about 30 mm from the end: a long, shallow oblique cut along the top of the cane, taking the top quarter off. The cut should taper from nothing at 30 mm to the full top surface at the tip.
Make shoulder cuts on each side of the exposed surface to narrow the nib width. For a broad-edge pen, aim for 3 to 6 mm across the tip.
Place the nib flat side down on the cutting mat. Cut straight across the tip to make the writing edge, a single clean slice perpendicular to the length.
Split the centre: press the tip of the knife gently down the centre line of the nib, 5 to 8 mm back from the tip, to create the ink-flow slit.

Dip the pen in ink and write a few test strokes on cartridge paper. The bamboo will absorb ink more slowly than metal and more uniformly than a quill. If the ink floods, the slit is too long. If the pen scratches, the writing edge is uneven, trim it again, cleanly and straight across.
A reed pen produces a slightly textured stroke because the material is not perfectly smooth. This texture is characteristic and desirable in informal large-scale work.

items.symptom: The cane splits along the grain during cutting
items.cause: The bamboo is too dry or the blade is not sharp enough.
items.fix: Soak the tip of the cane in water for 20 minutes before cutting. This softens the fibres and reduces splitting.

items.symptom: Ink does not flow at all
items.cause: The hollow interior of the bamboo is blocked by the node.
items.fix: Check that the cut end is on the open section between two nodes, not through a node. The hollow must extend up into the pen barrel.
